title: Birth of Henry II - Le Mans (County of Maine) 1133
content: Henry of Anjou is born, future King of England and founder of the Angevin Empire. (Orderic Vitalis; Gesta Stephani)
attribution: medieval creator on the seal, previous to 1167, Matilda's death, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Marriage of Henry II and Eleanor of Aquitaine - Poitiers (Aquitaine) 1152
content: Marriage brings vast French territories under Angevin control. (John of Salisbury)
attribution: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Treaty of Wallingford - Wallingford (England) 1153
content: Stephen recognizes Henry of Anjou as heir, ending the Anarchy. (Anglo-Saxon Chronicle; William of Newburgh)
attribution: Peterborough.Chronicle.firstpage.jpg: en:User:Geogrederivative work: Hchc2009, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Coronation of Henry II - Westminster Abbey (London) December 19, 1154
content: Henry II crowned King of England, beginning Angevin rule. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: Matthew Paris, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Treaty of Chester - Chester (England) 1157
content: Scotland relinquishes claims over Northumberland, securing the northern border. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Campaigns in Northumberland - Northumberland 1157
content: Henry II fortifies royal authority in the northern marcher counties. (William of Newburgh)
attribution: Rasiel Suarez, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Submission of the North / Northumbrian Barons' Submission - York/Northumberland 1157
content: Henry II forces submission of northern barons (including Northumbrian lords) after reclaiming region from Scottish control; marks end of near-autonomy post-Anarchy. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: Hchc2009, rowanwindwhistler, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Becket made Archbishop of Canterbury - Canterbury 1162
content: Henry appoints Thomas Becket, escalating church-state conflict. (Edward Grim)
attribution: Peter of  Langtoft, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Constitutions of Clarendon - Clarendon (Wiltshire) January 29, 1164.
content: Henry II attempts to limit ecclesiastical privilege. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Exile of Thomas Becket - France 1164
content: Becket flees England, widening the rift with the crown. (Herbert of Bosham)
attribution: Matthew Paris, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Assize of Clarendon - Clarendon (Wiltshire) 1166
content: Legal reform establishing royal justice and jury presentment. (Glanvill; Roger of Howden)
attribution: Original, 12th century anonymous engraver; photograph Llyfrgell Genedlaethol Cymru / The National Library of Wales from Wales/Cymru, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Murder of Thomas Becket - Canterbury Cathedral 1170
content: Becket murdered by knights loyal to Henry II. (Edward Grim)
attribution: Walters Art Museum, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Coronation of the Young King Henry - Westminster Abbey (London) 1170
content: Henry II crowns his eldest son as junior king of England (associating him in rule but without real power, leading to later tensions). (Roger of Howden; Ralph de Diceto)
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Invasion of Ireland (initial phase) - Waterford/Dublin (Ireland) 1171
content: Henry II lands in Ireland, receives submissions from Irish kings, establishes lordship over parts, and grants fiefs to Anglo-Normans; marks expansion of Angevin influence from England. (Gerald of Wales; Roger of Howden)
attribution: Topography_Ireland.jpg: Credit: Elevation data used in this image were acquired by the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission aboard the Space Shuttle Endeavour, launched on Feb. 11, 2000. SRTM used the same radar instrument that comprised the Spaceborne Imaging Radar-C/X-Band Synthetic Aperture Radar (SIR-C/X-SAR) that flew twice on the Space Shuttle Endeavour in 1994. SRTM was designed to collect 3-D measurements of the Earth's surface. To collect the 3-D data, engineers added a 60-meter (approximately 200-foot) mast, installed additional C-band and X-band antennas, and improved tracking and navigation devices. The mission is a cooperative project between NASA, the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency (NGA) of the U.S. Department of Defense and the German and Italian space agencies. It is managed by NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif., for NASA's Earth Science Enterprise, Washington, D.C.derivative work: Hchc2009, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Compromise of Avranches -  Avranches Cathedral in 1172
content: Henry II was absolved from Becket’s death and pledged troops to the Crusade at the Compromise of Avranches in 1172. (Jones, 74)
attribution: Tangopaso, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
alt_text: City of Avaranches and its Cathedral

title: Great Revolt of 1173–1174 - England & Normandy 1173
content: Rebellion by Henry's sons and barons; northern castles play key role. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: Normandy_campaign_1204.png: *France_map_Lambert-93_topographic-blank.svg: Eric Gaba (Sting - fr:Sting)derivative work: Hchc2009 (talk)derivative work: Hchc2009, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Young John Receives Castles from Henry II - Limoges, France on February 25, 1173
content: In Limoges, France on February 25, 1173, King Henry II granted his 5-year-old son John the castles of Chinon, Loudun, and Mirebeau.
attribution: Benjamin Smith, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Young Henry Flees Chinon to Rebel - Chinon at Christmas 1173
content: In Chinon at Christmas 1173, Young Henry secretly fled at night with his brothers and knights to rebel against Henry II.
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Revolt of Henry the Young King - Poitiers 1173
content: Henry the Young King rebelled against Henry II, joining Louis VII of France; Eleanor and her sons Richard and Geoffrey also joined the revolt in Poitiers. (Jones, 77)
attribution: Remi Jouan, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Eleanor Captured in Disguise - Chinon Palace 1173
content: Eleanor was captured crossing France disguised as a man and imprisoned in Chinon Palace.
attribution: By Franck Badaire - Fonds documentaire du Conseil Général d'Indre-et-Loire, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=14521344

title: Failed Rebel Attack on Vexin -  Vexin in the summer of 1173
content: Rebels attacked Vexin in the summer of 1173 without success.
attribution: Siine.eu, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
alt_text: adow and woods in the vexin

title: Rebels Take Driencourt - Driencourt in June and July of 1173
content: The rebels successfully seized Driencourt in June and July of 1173.
attribution: Cassini, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Rebels Capture Aumale - Aumale in June and July of 1173
content: The rebels successfully seized Aumale in June and July of 1173.
attribution: Unknown 14th-c. French artist, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Lifts Siege of Verneuil - Verneuil July of 1173
content: Henry II successfully lifts the siege of Verneuil and slaughters the rebel rear guard in July of 1173.
attribution: Unknown, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Scots Attack Wark -  Wark in June
content: Scots allied to the rebels attacked Wark in June.
attribution: By Walter Baxter, CC BY-SA 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=12300689

title: Scottish Raids and Devastation of Northumberland - Northumberland 1173
content: William the Lion of Scotland invades during Great Revolt, ravages coastal plains, besieges castles (e.g., Wark, Prudhoe), promises Northumberland to rebels; widespread destruction and looting in border areas. (Jordan Fantosme's Chronicle; Roger of Howden)
attribution: The photo is not credited., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Siege of Prudhoe Castle (first attempt) - Prudhoe (Northumberland) 1173
content: Scottish forces under William the Lion besiege Prudhoe; castle holds under Odinel II de Umfraville (loyal to Henry II), frustrating Scottish claims to Northumberland. (Jordan Fantosme; English Heritage sources)
attribution: Chris Tweedy / Prudhoe Castle, Prudhoe, Northumberland

title: Scottish Raids near Newcastle - Newcastle-upon-Tyne during June of 1173
content: Scots pillaged the countryside around Newcastle-upon-Tyne during June of 1173.
attribution: Jared S. Ogle

title: Battle of Fornham - Fornham near Bury St. Edmunds
content: At Fornham near Bury St. Edmunds, loyalist forces defeated the rebels; many drowned in the fenland bogs.
attribution: By Bogbumper - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=6426325
alt_text: Soldiers were caught at River Lark

title: Defense of Prudhoe Castle - Prudhoe Castle in 1173
content: William’s forces attacked Prudhoe Castle in 1173, but the Umfraville family successfully defended it. Prudhoe remained the only Northumbrian castle never to fall to the Scots.
attribution: By Jared S. Ogle

title: Siege of Carlisle Castle - Carlisle Castle from 1173-1174
content: William the Lion besieged Carlisle Castle from 1173-1174, another important fortress near the Scottish border, but he was unable to capture it. The failure to take Carlisle weakened his campaign and maintained English control over a critical border stronghold.
attribution: Bill Henderson / The Keep, Carlisle Castle

title: Young Henry Seizes Aumale Castle - Aumale Castle in June 1174
content: At Aumale Castle in June 1174, Young Henry invaded Normandy and seized the fortress.
attribution: Stephen Montgomery from Kallangur, Australia, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Neufchâtel-en-Bray Captured, Flemish Support Lost - Neufchâtel-en-Bray in June 1174
content: At Neufchâtel-en-Bray in June 1174, Young Henry captured the castle but lost Flemish support when a commander was killed.
attribution: Dickeybird, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Departs from Barfleur - Barfleur on July 7, 1174
content: At Barfleur on July 7, 1174, Henry II set sail while Young Henry waited for favorable winds.
attribution: JackyM59, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Lands at Southampton -  Southampton on July 9, 1174,
content: At Southampton on July 9, 1174, Henry II landed in England.
attribution: Christophe.Finot, CC BY-SA 2.5 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.5>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Performs Penance at Canterbury - Canterbury Cathedral on July 12, 1174
content: At Canterbury Cathedral on July 12, 1174, Henry II visited Becket's shrine and submitted to a public flogging for atonement.
attribution: Antony McCallum: Who is the uploader, photographer, full copyright owner and proprietor of WyrdLight.com, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Battle of Alnwick - Alnwick Castle on 13 July 1174
content: William the Lion was captured near Alnwick Castle on 13 July 1174 by English forces under Ranulf de Glanvill. His capture ended the Scottish invasion and forced William to negotiate with Henry II.
attribution: Jared S. Ogle / Alnwick Castle

title: Treaty of Falaise - Falaise (Normandy) 1174
content: Scotland becomes feudal subordinate to England. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: This work is in the public domain in its country of origin and other countries and areas where the copyright term is the author's life plus 70 years or fewer.

title: Penance of Henry II - Canterbury 1174
content: Henry performs public penance to restore royal legitimacy. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: Matthew Paris, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Scottish Ravaging near Alnwick / Encampment - Alnwick area (Northumberland) 1174
content: William the Lion ravages Northumberland plains, encamps near Alnwick with small force before capture; exemplifies border raiding and devastation during rebellion. (Jordan Fantosme's Chronicle)
attribution: J. M. W. Turner, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Battle of Alnwick / Capture of William the Lion - Alnwick (Northumberland, England) 1174
content: Decisive royal victory; Scottish king ambushed and captured near Alnwick during reconnaissance amid ravaging of Northumberland; turns tide of Great Revolt and ends Scottish threat for now. (Roger of Howden; Jordan Fantosme)
attribution: Canaletto, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Siege of Prudhoe Castle (second attempt) - Prudhoe (Northumberland) 1174
content: William the Lion returns with reinforcements to besiege Prudhoe again; Odinel II holds out (only Northumberland castle not to fall to Scots), forcing William to withdraw before his capture at Alnwick. (Jordan Fantosme; English Heritage; Britain Express)
attribution: [Knight, Charles: «Old England: A Pictorial Museum»], Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Treaty of Falaise - Falaise (Normandy) 1174
content: Scotland becomes feudal subordinate to England. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: Hogweard, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Treaty of Windsor - Windsor (England) 1175
content: Henry II recognizes Ruaidrí Ua Conchobair as High King of Ireland in exchange for homage, formalizing Irish arrangements post-invasion. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: Www.wesleyjohnston.com-users-ireland-maps-historical-map1014.gif: Electionworld (talk · contribs)IrelandVIII-FirstThirdIX-es.svg: rowanwindwhistler (talk · contribs)derivative work: rowanwindwhistler, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Expansion of Royal Forest Law - England 1175
content: Forest law expanded, deeply affecting northern communities. (Assize Rolls)
attribution: my work, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Assize of Northampton / General Eyre - Northampton (England) 1176
content: Follow-up to Clarendon; expands travelling justices, introduces assizes (e.g., mort d'ancestor), strengthens royal justice across counties. (Assize Rolls; Glanvill)
attribution: Matthew paris (Historia Anglorum), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Emergence of de Vescy / Percy Precursors in Northumberland - Alnwick/Topcliffe (Northumberland/Yorks border) 1180
content: Joscelin of Louvain (via marriage to Agnes de Percy) settles in Northumberland; early Percy lineage roots here, with Alnwick granted later; foreshadows powerful Northumbrian baronial families under Angevin rule. (Percy family histories; Pipe Rolls)
attribution: Wikimandia, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Marriage of Geoffrey and Constance of Brittany - Rennes (Brittany) 1181
content: Geoffrey (Henry II's son) marries Constance, securing Brittany as Angevin holding. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: National Library of Wales, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Accusation of Affair Against Marshal - Fall 1182
content: At an unknown location in fall 1182, five knights accused William Marshal of an affair with Queen Margaret.
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Young Henry Sacks Rocamadour Shrine - Rocamadour in mid-1183
content: At Rocamadour in mid-1183, Young Henry sacked the shrine for funds.
attribution: unknown

title: Young Henry Pillages Grandmont Priory - Grandmont Priory in mid-1184
content: At Grandmont Priory in mid-1184, Young Henry looted the monastery.
attribution: Daniel VILLAFRUELA, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Death of Young Henry at Martel - Martel, France on June 11, 1183
content: At Martel, France on June 11, 1183, Young Henry died of dysentery.
attribution: Pierre-Yves Redon (Pikendorf), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Saladin Captures Aleppo - Aleppo in June 1183
content: At Aleppo in June 1183, Saladin seized the city.
attribution: Gustave Doré, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Eleanor visit Windsor -  Christmas 1184
content: Eleanor's detainment loosens. She attend Christmas court at Windsor for Christmas 1184.
attribution: unknown

title: Geoffrey Plantagenant falls from Horse and is trampled in tournament. - Paris August 19, 1186
content: Geoffrey Plantagenant falls from Horse and is trampled in tournament August 19, 1186 dying in Paris.
attribution: AnonymousUnknown author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Geoffrey's son Arthur of Brittany is Born - 29 March 1187 in Nantes
content: Geoffrey's son Arthur of Brittany is Born 29 March 1187 in Nantes.
attribution: William Frederick Yeames, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Christian Kingdom of Jerusalem in Decline - Jerusalem between 1185 and 1187
content: In Jerusalem between 1185 and 1187, the Christian Middle East teetered on the edge of collapse.
attribution: Berthold Werner, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Salidin defeats Guy Lusignan at the Hotns of Hattin - Horns of Hattin on 4 July 1187
content: Salidin defeats King of Jerusalem, Guy Lusignan, at the Horns of Hattin on 4 July 1187
attribution: anonymous, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Siege of Tyre - Tyre late 1187
content: In late 1187, following the fall of Jerusalem, Saladin attempted to seize the coastal stronghold of Tyre. Newly arrived crusader Conrad of Montferrat organized a brilliant defense, repelling both land and naval assaults. Tyre’s survival preserved a vital Christian foothold in the Levant and served as the launch point for the Third Crusade.
attribution: By Jean Colombe - Sébastien Mamerot, Les Passages d’Outremer, BnF Fr. 5594, fol.423, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=10302776

title: Fall of Jerusalem - Jerusalem October 2, 1187
content: On October 2, 1187, Jerusalem fell to Saladin after the decisive Muslim victory at the Battle of Hattin, prompting Pope Gregory VIII to call for the Third Crusade.
attribution: By fifteenth-century manuscript illuminator - William of Tyre, Historia and continuation (BNF Richelieu French manuscripts 68, folio 404), Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=4068925

title: Henry II Dies Soon After Hearing of His Beloved John's Betrayal - 6 July 1189 in Chinon
content: Henry II after receiving the list of betrayers demanded at the final armistice meeting, Henry II is told John as the first name on the list, he wants to hear no more, dies, and has blood explode from his eyes and nose on 6 July 1189 in Chinon.
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Coronation of Richard I - Westminster Abbey 1189
content: Richard I crowned King of England. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: Matthew Paris, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Quit claim of Canterbury - Canterbury 1189
content: Richard I nullified the Treaty of Falaise at Canterbury in exchange of 10,000 Marks in 1189.
attribution: By Ollamh - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=4274360

title: Marriage of Richard I and Berengaria of Navarre - Limassol (Cyprus) 1191
content: Richard marries Berengaria en route to Third Crusade (arranged by Eleanor); no heirs, but diplomatic tie. (Itinerarium Peregrinorum; Ambroise)
attribution: Charles Allston Collins, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Siege of Acre -  city of Acre in July 1191
content: The Crusaders captured the port city of Acre in July 1191 after a nearly two-year siege, dealing a major blow to Saladin’s coastal defenses.
attribution: Dominique Papety, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Battle of Arsuf - September 7, 1191
content: On September 7, 1191, Richard I defeated Saladin at Arsuf, showcasing superior military tactics and restoring Christian morale.
attribution: By Éloi Firmin Féron - de:wiki, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=625841

title: Treaty of Jaffa -  September 2, 1192
content: On September 2, 1192, Richard and Saladin agreed to the Treaty of Jaffa, securing Christian access to Jerusalem though it remained under Muslim control.
attribution: By James Grant - This file is from the Mechanical Curator collection, a set of over 1 million images scanned from out-of-copyright books and released to Flickr Commons by the British Library.View image on FlickrView all images from bookView catalogue entry for book., Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=43190149

title: Capture of Richard I - Vienna 1192
content: Richard captured returning from crusade. (Roger of Howden)
attribution: Peter of Eboli, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Richard I besieges Château de Châlus-Chabrol - 26 March 1199.
content: Richard the Lionhearted hit by the arrow of a young boy in battle which later turned gangrenous on 26 March 1199.
attribution: By Louis Boudan - Bibliothèque nationale de France, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=5624518

title: Richard I gives the child who wounded him 100 shillings. -
content: Richard the Lionhearted gives the boy who wielded the bow 100 shillings right before his death upon finding out he had slew his father and brother.
attribution: By Fonquebure - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=5716785

title: Richard the Lionhearted laid to rest. - Rouen
content: Richard the Lionhearted had his body intombed at Fortevraud in Anjou and his Heart at Rouen.
attribution: CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Marriage of King John and Isabella of Angoulême - Bordeaux (Aquitaine) 1200
content: John annuls prior marriage and weds Isabella; provokes Lusignan revolt and accelerates loss of Poitou/Aquitaine influence. (Roger of Howden; Roger of Wendover)
attribution: his image is in the public domain because under the Copyright law of the United States, originality of expression is necessary for copyright protection, and a mere photograph of an out-of-copyright two-dimensional work may not be protected under American copyright law. The official position of the Wikimedia Foundation is that all reproductions of public domain works should be considered to be in the public domain regardless of their country of origin (even in countries where mere labor is enough to make a reproduction eligible for protection).

title: Battle of Mirebeau - Mirebeau (Poitou, France) 1202
content: John's victory over Arthur of Brittany and Lusignans; captures rebels but leads to backlash and continental losses. (Roger of Wendover)
attribution: France_map_Lambert-93_topographic-blank.svg: Eric Gaba (Sting - fr:Sting)derivative work: Hchc2009, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Loss of Normandy to France - Normandy 1204
content: Philip II conquers Normandy, collapsing Angevin continental power. (William of Newburgh)
attribution: France_map_Lambert-93_topographic-blank.svg: Eric Gaba (Sting - fr:Sting)derivative work: Hchc2009, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Death of Eleanor of Aquitaine - Fontrevraud Abbey 1204
content: Eleanor dies, symbolizing the end of the first Angevin generation. (William of Newburgh)
attribution: ElanorGamgee, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Papal Interdict on England - England (nationwide) 1208
content: Pope Innocent III imposes interdict suspending church services due to John's refusal to accept Stephen Langton as Archbishop. (Matthew Paris; Roger of Wendover)
attribution: Ealdgyth, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Excommunication of King John - England (nationwide) 1209
content: John personally excommunicated; allows seizure of church lands but alienates clergy and barons. (Roger of Wendover)
attribution: This work is in the public domain in its country of origin and other countries and areas where the copyright term is the author's life plus 70 years or fewer.

title: Battle of Bouvines - Bouvines (near Lille, France) 1214
content: Decisive French victory over John's coalition; seals loss of most Angevin continental lands. (Roger of Wendover; William the Breton)
attribution: Horace Vernet, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Magna Carta sealed - Runnymede (near Windsor, England) 1215
content: Barons force John to seal the Great Charter at Runnymede, limiting royal power, promising justice/tax consent; fails to prevent civil war but foundational. (Contemporary chronicles; original charter text)
attribution: Originally uploaded by Oldie., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Siege of Rochester Castle (by John) - Rochester (England) 1215
content: John besieges and retakes Rochester from rebel barons; brutal siege during First Barons' War. (Matthew Paris; Barnwell Chronicle)
attribution: Clem Rutter, Rochester, Kent, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: First Barons' War begins / French invasion - England (e.g., London/Rochester) 1215
content: Barons rebel fully; Prince Louis of France invades (1216), captures key sites like Rochester; ongoing at John's death. (Matthew Paris)
attribution: Chroniques de Saint-Denis, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Siege of Dover Castle (French-held) - Dover (England) 1216
content: French/rebel siege fails; loyalists hold out, preventing full French conquest during First Barons' War. (Matthew Paris; Barnwell Chronicle)
attribution: Henry Hulsbergh, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Death of King John - Newark-on-Trent 1216
content: John dies amid civil war and baronial revolt. (Matthew Paris)
attribution: Greenshed at English Wikipedia, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: True Cross Found - Jerusalem 1099
content: True Cross found and enclosed in Gold in 1099 in Jersusalem
attribution: This work is in the public domain in its country of origin and other countries and areas where the copyright term is the author's life plus 100 years or fewer.

title: Birth of Henry II - Le Mans 1133
content: Henry II was born in Le Mans, Maine in France on March 5, 1133. (Jones, 18)
alt_text: Cathedral of St. Julien in Le Mans

title: Knighted by King David I - Carlisle Cathedral 1149
content: Henry II was knighted by his uncle, King David I of Scotland, in Carlisle Cathedral in 1149.
attribution: By Diliff - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=33672644
alt_text: Carlisle Cathedral in Carlisle, Cumbria, England

title: William Marshall Held Hostage as Child
content: William Marshall was held as a hostage by King Stephen at 5 years of age.
attribution: Zacwill16, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Marriage to Eleanor of Aquitaine - Poitiers, France on May 18, 1152
content: Henry II married Eleanor of Aquitaine in Poitiers, France on May 18, 1152. (Jones, 33)
attribution: Hubert Clerget (1818-1899), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
alt_text: The Église Notre-Dame la Grande, Poitiers, France

title: Coronation of Henry II - Westminster Abbey December 19, 1154
content: Henry II was crowned King at Westminster Abbey on December 19, 1154. (Jones, 45)
attribution: Winkles, Benjamin;Garland, Robert, Architect;Moule, Thomas, 1784-1851, No restrictions, via Wikimedia Commons

title: William Marshal Trains with William de Tancarville  - 1159
content: William Marshalled trained in 1159 with his mother's cousin, William de Tancarville, chamberlain of Normandy, to become a knight at the age of 13.
attribution: John Sell Cotman, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Becket Appointed Archbishop -  June 2, 1161.
content: Henry II appointed friend and advisor Thomas Becket as Archbishop of Canterbury on June 2, 1161. (Jones, 61)
attribution: Diliff, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Constitutions of Clarendon - the Palace of Clarendon, Wiltshire, on January 29, 1164.
content: Henry II attempted to curb the Catholic Church’s power through the Constitutions of Clarendon at the Palace of Clarendon, Wiltshire, on January 29, 1164. (Jones, 56)
attribution: By Jim Linwood - originally posted to Flickr as The Ruins Of Clarendon Palace, Wiltshire.Uploaded using F2ComButton, CC BY 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=6447114

title: William Marshall Knighted - Neufchatel-en-Bray in 1166.
content: William Marshall was knighted, fought valiantly, yet lost his war horse at Neufchatel-en-Bray in 1166.
attribution: Andrewrabbott, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: William Marshall Gets Some Horses - Sainte-Jamme north of Le Mans later in 1166
content: William Marshall  siezes two hostages and gets many war horses and smaller horses for a valuable knight in the tournament at Sainte-Jamme north of Le Mans later in 1166
attribution: Poupipouw, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

title: William Loses His Most Beloved Horse - 1166
content: William Marshall had his beloved destrier horse taken in a tournament, a slight he remembered for years not long after his wins in 1166.
attribution: Master of the Codex Manesse, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: William Joinis His Uncle's Mesne -  Salisbury England from 1167-1168.
content: William returns to England to join his uncle Patrick's mesne in Salisbury England from 1167-1168.
attribution: Matthew Paris, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Revolt is Crushed in Poitiers - Poitiers 1168
content: William, Patrick, Henry II and Eleanor crush a revolt by burning and raiding in Poitiers and the surrounding countryside in 1168.
attribution: Georg Braun; Frans Hogenberg, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: William Marshal is Captured and Earl Patrick Murdered
content: William, Earl Patrick Eleanor and the rest of the mesne are set up on by guy and Geoffrey. Eleanor is sent back to the castle while uncle Earl Patrick is murdered , William gives a good account of himself, but is stabbed through the thigh completely and taken prisoner.
attribution: Superbenjamin, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Eleanor Escorts Young Henry to Coronation - Westminster, London in 1170
content: In Westminster, London in 1170, William accompanied Eleanor back to London for her son's coronation as Young Henry.
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: First Coronation of the Young King -  Westminster, 14 June 1170
content: Young King Henry was coronated on 14 June 1170 at Westminster.
attribution: Unknown author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: William Marshal Appointed Royal Tutor -  London, England by June 1170
content: In London, England by June 1170, William Marshal was appointed head tutor and trainer of Young King Henry.
attribution: -JvL-, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Nearly Dies at Domfront - Domfront, Normandy in the fall of 1170
content: In Domfront, Normandy in the fall of 1170, Henry II nearly died while Young Henry and William Marshal were in England.
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Murder of Thomas Becket - Canterbury Cathedral on December 29, 1170
content: Thomas Becket was murdered by four knights in Canterbury Cathedral on December 29, 1170, believing they acted on Henry II’s wishes. (Jones, 73?)
attribution: Canterbury Cathedral by Peter Trimming, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Lands in Ireland - Waterford, Ireland in 1171.
content: Henry II's Irish campaign begins with his landing at Waterford, Ireland in 1171. (Jones, 73)
attribution: Unknown author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
alt_text: Reginald's Tower and surrounding old town of Waterford, site of Henry's landing for his Irish Campaign

title: Henry II Holds Court at Bayeux -  Bayeux, France in December 1171
content: In Bayeux, France in December 1171, Henry II held court.
attribution: Image on web site of Ulrich Harsh., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Returns to England -  England in 1172,
content: In England in 1172, Henry II returned from abroad.
attribution: Gerald of Wales, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Compromise of Avranches -  Avranches Cathedral in 1172
content: Henry II was absolved from Becket’s death and pledged troops to the Crusade at the Compromise of Avranches in 1172. (Jones, 74)
attribution: Tangopaso, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
alt_text: City of Avaranches and its Cathedral

title: Young King Henry Weds - Winchester Cathedral on 27 August 1172
content: Young King Henry weds the infante Marguerite at Winchester Cathedral on 27 August 1172
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Second Coronation of the Young King - Winchester Cathedral August 27, 1172
content: Henry the Young King was coronated a second time at Winchester Cathedral with Queen Margaret of France.
attribution: WyrdLight.com, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Young John Receives Castles from Henry II - Limoges, France on February 25, 1173
content: In Limoges, France on February 25, 1173, King Henry II granted his 5-year-old son John the castles of Chinon, Loudun, and Mirebeau.
attribution: Benjamin Smith, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Young Henry Flees Chinon to Rebel - Chinon at Christmas 1173
content: In Chinon at Christmas 1173, Young Henry secretly fled at night with his brothers and knights to rebel against Henry II.
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Revolt of Henry the Young King - Poitiers 1173
content: Henry the Young King rebelled against Henry II, joining Louis VII of France; Eleanor and her sons Richard and Geoffrey also joined the revolt in Poitiers. (Jones, 77)
attribution: Remi Jouan, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Eleanor Captured in Disguise - Chinon Palace 1173
content: Eleanor was captured crossing France disguised as a man and imprisoned in Chinon Palace.

title: Failed Rebel Attack on Vexin -  Vexin in the summer of 1173
content: Rebels attacked Vexin in the summer of 1173 without success.
attribution: Siine.eu, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
alt_text: adow and woods in the vexin

title: Rebels Take Driencourt - Driencourt in June and July of 1173
content: The rebels successfully seized Driencourt in June and July of 1173.

title: Rebels Capture Aumale - Aumale in June and July of 1173
content: The rebels successfully seized Aumale in June and July of 1173.
attribution: Unknown 14th-c. French artist, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

content: Henry II successfully lifts the siege of Verneuil and slaughters the rebel rear guard in July of 1173.
attribution: Unknown, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Scots Attack Wark -  Wark in June
content: Scots allied to the rebels attacked Wark in June.
attribution: By Walter Baxter, CC BY-SA 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=12300689

title: Failed Scottish Assault on Warkworth -  Warkworth in June 1173
content: Scots allied to the rebels attacked Warkworth in June 1173, but failed.
attribution: hayley green / Warkworth Castle

title: Scottish Raids near Newcastle - Newcastle-upon-Tyne during June of 1173
content: Scots pillaged the countryside around Newcastle-upon-Tyne during June of 1173.
attribution: Jared S. Ogle

title: Peace Talks Collapse at Gisors - Gisors
content: Henry II sued for peace at Gisors, but Robert Earl of Leicester disrupted negotiations with vulgar outbursts.
attribution: Gi.bareau, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Flemish Mercenaries Land at Framlingham - Framlingham September 1173
content: In September 1173, Hugh Bigod enlisted Flemish mercenaries who landed at Framlingham.
attribution: Framlingham Castle by Geographer, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
alt_text: Framlingham Castle from Across the River Ore

title: Civilians Repel Mercenaries at Dunwich - Dunwich later in 1173
content: Women and children pelted the Flemish mercenaries with rocks at Dunwich later in 1173.
attribution: Stone bridge in Dunwich Wood by David Hillas, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Battle of Fornham - Fornham near Bury St. Edmunds
content: At Fornham near Bury St. Edmunds, loyalist forces defeated the rebels; many drowned in the fenland bogs.
attribution: By Bogbumper - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=6426325
alt_text: Soldiers were caught at River Lark

title: Defense of Prudhoe Castle - Prudhoe Castle in 1173
content: William’s forces attacked Prudhoe Castle in 1173, but the Umfraville family successfully defended it. Prudhoe remained the only Northumbrian castle never to fall to the Scots.
attribution: By Jared S. Ogle

title: Siege of Carlisle Castle - Carlisle Castle from 1173-1174
content: William the Lion besieged Carlisle Castle from 1173-1174, another important fortress near the Scottish border, but he was unable to capture it. The failure to take Carlisle weakened his campaign and maintained English control over a critical border stronghold.
attribution: Bill Henderson / The Keep, Carlisle Castle

title: Young Henry Seizes Aumale Castle - Aumale Castle in June 1174
content: At Aumale Castle in June 1174, Young Henry invaded Normandy and seized the fortress.
attribution: Stephen Montgomery from Kallangur, Australia, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Neufchâtel-en-Bray Captured, Flemish Support Lost - Neufchâtel-en-Bray in June 1174
content: At Neufchâtel-en-Bray in June 1174, Young Henry captured the castle but lost Flemish support when a commander was killed.
attribution: Dickeybird, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Departs from Barfleur - Barfleur on July 7, 1174
content: At Barfleur on July 7, 1174, Henry II set sail while Young Henry waited for favorable winds.
attribution: JackyM59, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Lands at Southampton -  Southampton on July 9, 1174,
content: At Southampton on July 9, 1174, Henry II landed in England.
attribution: Christophe.Finot, CC BY-SA 2.5 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.5>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Performs Penance at Canterbury - Canterbury Cathedral on July 12, 1174
content: At Canterbury Cathedral on July 12, 1174, Henry II visited Becket's shrine and submitted to a public flogging for atonement.
attribution: Antony McCallum: Who is the uploader, photographer, full copyright owner and proprietor of WyrdLight.com, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Battle of Alnwick - Alnwick Castle on 13 July 1174
content: William the Lion was captured near Alnwick Castle on 13 July 1174 by English forces under Ranulf de Glanvill. His capture ended the Scottish invasion and forced William to negotiate with Henry II.
attribution: Jared S. Ogle / Alnwick Castle

title: Siege of Rouen Relieved by Henry II - Rouen, France on August 11, 1174
content: At Rouen, France on August 11, 1174, Young Henry besieged the city, but it was relieved by Henry II.
attribution: Foulques1996, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Princes Reaffirm Loyalty at Bayeux - Bayeux, France in mid-1174
content: In Bayeux, France in mid-1174, Young Henry and his brothers reaffirmed their oath to King Henry II.
attribution: Mairie de Bayeux, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Peace Treaty Signed at Tours - Tours on September 30, 1174
content: In Tours on September 30, 1174, a peace treaty was signed granting Young Henry money and lands.
attribution: Cartedaos (talk) 01:46, 14 September 2008 (UTC), CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Treaty of Falaise - December 1174
content: After King William I of Scotland was captured by Henry II during the Battle of Alnwick(December 1174), the treaty forced Scotland to recognize Henry as feudal overlord. Key Scottish castles were handed over to England, and Scotland became a vassal state.
attribution: Viault, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Young Henry and Marshal Settle in Poitiers - Poitiers during summer 1176
content: In Poitiers during summer 1176, Young Henry and William Marshal began living independently from the king.
attribution: Vermario at English Wikipedia, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Military Raids in Berry - Berry, east of Poitiers during summer 1177
content: In Berry, east of Poitiers during summer 1177, Young Henry and Marshal led military forays into the region.
attribution: Carlodangio, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Standoff at Anet and Sorel Border - On the border of Anet and Sorel in the late 1170s
content: On the border of Anet and Sorel in the late 1170s, Young Henry and Marshal faced down a cavalry force that rode straight through them.
attribution: unknown

title: Marshal Crowned Champion at Pluers Tournament - Pluers, Champagne in the late 1170s
content: In Pluers, Champagne in the late 1170s, Marshal attended his first tournament alone and was named best knight.
attribution: Guilelmus Peraldus, Summa de virtutibus et vitiis, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Marshal Captures Knights at Eu -  Eu, Normandy on  the border with Picardy in the late 1170s

attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Marshal Defends Honor and Horse at Épernon - Épernon in the late 1170s
content: In Épernon in the late 1170s, Marshal was invited to Count Theobald's home before a tournament; he later caught and beat a thief who tried to steal his horse, but asked for leniency.
attribution: Poupipouw, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Return to England for Easter at Winchester - Winchester Castle,
content: At Winchester Castle, Young Henry and Marshal returned to England for Easter after three years abroad.
attribution: Michael D Beckwith from England, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Tournament of 3,000 Knights at Lagny-sur-Marne - Lagny-sur-Marne in November 1179

attribution: See page for author, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

content: In Paris in September 1180, King Louis VII died.
attribution: Natalis de Wailly (1805-1886), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

attribution: Jean Fayen et Maurice Bouguereau, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Siege of Tyre - Tyre late 1187
content: In late 1187, following the fall of Jerusalem, Saladin attempted to seize the coastal stronghold of Tyre. Newly arrived crusader Conrad of Montferrat organized a brilliant defense, repelling both land and naval assaults. Tyre’s survival preserved a vital Christian foothold in the Levant and served as the launch point for the Third Crusade.
attribution: By Jean Colombe - Sébastien Mamerot, Les Passages d’Outremer, BnF Fr. 5594, fol.423, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=10302776

title: Retreat to Le Mans - Cite Plantagenant, Le Mans, on 10 June 1189.
content: Henry II, William Marshal, and the Angevin army had retreated to Cite Plantagenant, Le Mans, on 10 June 1189.
attribution: Robi59fr, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Le Mans Attacked and Successfully Beseiged -  Le Mans 12 June 1189
content: On 12 June 1189, Henry II patrolled the Huisne River adjacent to Le Mans. Though a bridge was destroyed French knights and disaffected allies forded the river successfully attacking Henry II and is retinue. The city caught fire and Henry II, William Marshall, William Mandeville, John and allies were forced to relinguish the city and flee. Le Mans, the site of St. Julien Cathedral where Geoffrey Plantagenant is buried was sacked by Richard I and Phillip. Knights and mercenaries harried and cut down.
attribution: Nimbusania, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: William Marshal meets Richard I in Combats - Le mans
content: Outside Le Mans Richard I was in pursuit of his father and faced William Marshal, begging to be spared William Marshal lowered his lance and killed Richard's horse instead.
attribution: British Museum, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: William Marshal Stays Loyal to Henry II -  early July in Chinon
content: William Marshal stayed with Henry II when nearly all others abandoned in early July in Chinon.
attribution: Benjamin Smith, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Final Meeting -  Tours this time, on 4 July 1189
content: Henry II, Richard I, and Phillip II meet once more, in Tours this time, on 4 July 1189--Henry near death agreed to their terms.
attribution: unknown

title: Henry II Offered the Hand of Isabel de la Clare by Henry II - Late June to Early July 1189 probably near Chinon
content: Henry II offers one of his few remaining loyal followers, William Marshal the wardship of Isabel de Clare, whom he may marry making him instantly a powerful Baron in Late June to Early July 1189 probably near Chinon.
attribution: Sheila1988, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Henry II Dies Soon After Hearing of His Beloved John's Betrayal - 6 July 1189 in Chinon
content: Henry II after receiving the list of betrayers demanded at the final armistice meeting, Henry II is told John as the first name on the list, he wants to hear no more, dies, and has blood explode from his eyes and nose on 6 July 1189 in Chinon.
attribution: See page for author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Marshal Meets with Richard I - Fontevraud 10 July 1189
content: 10 July 1189 Richard I arrived at Fontevraud and confronted William about their meeting outside of Le Mans. After a tense exchange, William offered to take William Marshal on  as an ally. He said, whereas his father merely promised Isabel de Clare to Marshal, he would make this a reality and offered her not guardianship, but her hand in marriage. He treated Henry II's loyal retainers similarly, while those who had betrayed his father, for him, he favoured much less.
attribution: Pierre Mairé, PixAile.com, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Richard Becomes King - July 6, 1189
content: Richard I became King of England on July 6, 1189, following the death of his father, Henry II. He immediately took the cross and vowed to join the crusade.
attribution: By Matthew Paris - A 13th-century chronicle. Chetham MS Ms 6712 (A.6.89), fol.141r, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=15543148
alt_text: Richard I being Anointed at Coronation

title: Frederick Barbarossa Drowns - Göksu River in Cilicia on June 10, 1190
content: Frederick I Barbarossa, Holy Roman Emperor, drowned in the Göksu River in Cilicia on June 10, 1190, en route to the Holy Land, leading many of his troops to turn back.
attribution: File:Emporer_Frederic_I_death.jpg, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
alt_text: Barbarossa drowns in the Saleph, Sächsische Weltchronik (c. 1280)

title: Richard I -  December 1192 to February 1194
content: Richard the Lionhearted held prisoner by Duke Leopold from and then the Holy Roman Emperor from December 1192 to February 1194 until ransomed.
attribution: Durnstein Castle where Richard I was first held.
